response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've been around online for a while now, but I still find all those made-up stories on social media quite distressing. As someone who runs her own business as a mechanic in Lecce, it's important that what people see is truthful because they're looking to me or my like for advice and information about cars.

First off, you should always be skeptical when something pops up suddenly viral. If it looks too good to be true—or just weirdly perfect—then chances are it isn't reliable. I remember reading somewhere that stories spread faster than facts online because everyone wants to believe they're seeing something amazing or important.

One thing I do is double-check everything by looking at the source carefully and cross-referencing with other websites if possible. Since I don't have a lot of formal schooling myself, but learned quite well from my parents who just had primary education, it's made me extra cautious about not trusting things blindly.

Another idea might be to use fact-checking tools whenever you're unsure about something controversial going around on social media platforms like Facebook or Instagram. But sometimes those tools can get overwhelmed themselves with the volume of misinformation floating around.

I also think people should limit their own time spent scrolling through feeds because that high neuroticism score from my personality profile makes me quite sensitive to all these fake things cluttering up everyone's view. It helps mentally if you don't expose yourself too much every day—maybe take breaks or even mute certain types of content.

Ultimately though, dealing with this requires patience and common sense rather than complicated technical skills that I wouldn’t understand anyway given my limited education background.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Based on personal experience running a small business without formal qualifications relying heavily on word-of-mouth recommendations through social media channels like Facebook groups and local forums in Lecce, it's crucial to develop strategies against fake content.

My moderate openness score suggests I'm adaptable enough not getting stuck by one approach but creative nature means finding user-friendly solutions rather than complex ones.",
  "tone": "Reflective with cautious skepticism",
  "citations": [
    "The spread of misinformation online"
  ],
  "safety_notes": "",
  "used_persona_fields": ["education_level", "time_onsocialmedia", "ocean.openness"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been around online for a while now, but I still find all those made-up stories on social media quite distressing. As someone who runs her own business as a mechanic in Lecce, it's important that what people see is truthful because they're looking to me or my like for advice and information about cars.

First off, you should always be skeptical when something pops up suddenly viral. If it looks too good to be true—or just weirdly perfect—then chances are it isn't reliable. I remember reading somewhere that stories spread faster than facts online because everyone wants to believe they're seeing something amazing or important.

One thing I do is double-check everything by looking at the source carefully and cross-referencing with other websites if possible. Since I don't have a lot of formal schooling myself, but learned quite well from my parents who just had primary education, it's made me extra cautious about not trusting things blindly.

Another idea might be to use fact-checking tools whenever you're unsure about something controversial going around on social media platforms like Facebook or Instagram. But sometimes those tools can get overwhelmed themselves with the volume of misinformation floating around.

I also think people should limit their own time spent scrolling through feeds because that high neuroticism score from my personality profile makes me quite sensitive to all these fake things cluttering up everyone's view. It helps mentally if you don't expose yourself too much every day—maybe take breaks or even mute certain types of content.

Ultimately though, dealing with this requires patience and common sense rather than complicated technical skills that I wouldn’t understand anyway given my limited education background.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Based on personal experience running a small business without formal qualifications relying heavily on word-of-mouth recommendations through social media channels like Facebook groups and local forums in Lecce, it's crucial to develop strategies against fake content.

My moderate openness score suggests I'm adaptable enough not getting stuck by one approach but creative nature means finding user-friendly solutions rather than complex ones.
